w:document.mc:Ignorable: w14 w15 w16se w16cid w16 w16cex w16sdtdh w16sdtfl w16du wp14

w:r.w:t: Design specification, calculation engine, master data, quotation flow, and build plan
Status: all design decisions locked. Build proceeds on Phase 0.
Audience: Director (executive summary) + Development team (technical depth)
Project: RTPH Commercial Data Sheet (CDS) — replaces the legacy Multiply costing template and the HRS

This document supersedes all earlier flow documents. It is the single canonical reference for master data, calculation engine, quotation lifecycle, and v1 build plan. Each section cites the project file or sheet it is built from.
All design decisions locked. Build proceeds on Phase 0.
v1, v2 of this document; flow_Edited.docx
Director: §1 Executive Summary, §2 Operating Principles, §9 Quotation Lifecycle, §15 Locked Decisions. ~10 minutes.
Engineering lead: §3–§8 (architecture, masters, engine, belt matrix) and §13 Database Schema. ~60 minutes.
Developer on a section: jump to the relevant master in §6 or component in §7. Cross-references take you to the database row, the form field, and the validating Excel cell.
runs belt costing today on two Excel files: a per-belt-type costing template and the HRS
(7,000+ rows, 108 columns). Both hardcode rates inside formulas. Both silently zero out costs when a lookup misses.
This spec defines the replacement — a web application that produces the same outputs the team produces today, with three structural upgrades that change the economics:
Masters are the single source of truth. No rate, SG, or wastage is typed inside a formula. Every number
in a master row. Update once; all future quotes follow.
One engine for every belt type. The 14 variants collapse into 5 calculation families. A belt type declares its components; the engine sums what is declared. Build the core once.
. The moment a quote is sent, every rate it used is snapshotted onto the line. Master changes after that do not rewrite history. Revisions are new versions.
12 belt types: Multi-Ply, Multi-Ply with Breaker (the validated pilot), Bag Diverter, Bucket Elevator, Straight Warp, Rough Top, Wavy Top, Endless, Jointless, Chevron, Sidewall, Steep Angle.
2 belt types in v2: Pipe (tube geometry) and Paper Reel (specification pending).
Full quotation lifecycle: customer → quotation header → multiple belt lines → Standard + VD pricing → discount → final → send (freeze) → revisions → optional PO tracking → won/lost.
Outputs: customer-facing branded PDF, internal cost-sheet view, optional shop-floor job card.
What is fixed versus legacy Excel
Eight critical bugs become acceptance criteria (full list in §12). The headline ones:
Width wastage is now Edge-Master driven (Cut +30 mm,
+0). Today the Multiply sheet adds 30 mm regardless of edge.
Endless belt RMC now divides by length. Today it doesn’t — Reverse GP% comes out meaningless.
Steep Angle blinker toggle now drives the calculation. Today the toggle is cosmetic — cost is always added.
Fabric-style lookups no longer fall through silently above range. Today, an EP-800/5 belt zeros out its fabric cost.
Skim compound becomes a first-class selectable input. Today it is bundled into the grade with no dropdown.
Formulations App. When integration ships, a raw-material price change in a
every compound that uses it, and every future belt quote that uses those compounds.
Auditable margin. Order Rate vs frozen RMC is one query. Win-loss, customer profitability, and grade-family margin are derivable in seconds.
No silent under-costing. Every master lookup either succeeds or hard-blocks the save. The class of bug where a missing row turns into ₹0 of cost is gone.

All 10 design questions raised during spec review are now locked (see §15). The build has a deterministic target. Items LD-6, LD-7, and LD-8 expect short downstream confirmations when their belt type or feature is built — none of them blocks Phase 0 or Phase 1.
Three principles drive every design choice here. If a later section appears to bend one of these, treat it as a bug in this document and flag it.
Principle 1 — Masters are the single source of truth

No rate, specific gravity, wastage percentage, freight rate, packing cost, COP rate, or breaker GSM is ever typed inside a calculation. Every such number is a column on a master row. The engine reads it by lookup.
A master change takes effect on the next quotation. In-flight quotations that are still DRAFT or PRICED recompute live. Quotations that have been SENT remain frozen on their snapshot.

if a quote cannot be saved because a master row is missing (no Fabric SKU at the chosen per-ply rating, no freight zone for the chosen destination), the system shows the gap and refuses to save. It never silently substitutes zero. This eliminates the most damaging class of legacy bug — a missing row producing a hugely
A belt type does not have its own calculation. It declares its components; the engine sums those. Five calculation families in v1, plus one in v2:
Multi-Ply, Straight Warp, Rough Top, Wavy Top, Endless, Jointless
Base + Breaker Top and/or Bottom + their skims
Multi-Ply with Breaker, Bag Diverter, Bucket Elevator
Base + Cleats + Sidewalls + Blinkers + Solution + Hardener
the form shows only the component sections declared by the chosen belt type. A Sidewall belt’s form never shows cleat inputs. A
belt’s form never shows blinker inputs. The legacy Excel had the opposite — every belt-type sheet inherited stray cells from siblings (audit bug X5).

A quotation line in DRAFT or PRICED recomputes live whenever any master rate changes. The instant the parent quotation is marked SENT, every rate the line depends on is copied onto the line as a frozen snapshot — compound price, SG, fabric price, freight, packing, COP, GP%, discount, exchange rate. The line then shows exactly what was quoted, even if the master is later changed or the compound archived.
revisions do not overwrite. A revision creates a new versioned line (Revised-1, Revised-2, …) with its own fresh snapshot. The previous version remains visible. The HRS
“four revision columns” become proper versioned records, with no limit on revision count and a full audit trail.
No rounding. Selling prices show exactly as computed. The legacy
Standard + VD always shown side-by-side. Sales picks per line, applies discount, and the final price is the headline.
The load-bearing fabric core of the belt — one or more plies of woven fabric.
One layer of carcass fabric. A 5-ply belt has 5 fabric layers.
Rubber layer above the carcass — the carrying side, in contact with material.
Rubber layer below the carcass — the pulley side.
Thin rubber coating bonding fabric plies to each other and to the cover. One per belt (the “carcass skim”). Breakers carry their own skim layers in addition.
Optional reinforcement layer between carcass and cover, for impact protection. May be present on top, on bottom, on both, or absent. Fabric or steel.
Raised rubber bar across the belt (Chevron, Steep Angle) to stop material rolling back on inclines.
Corrugated rubber wall along belt edges (Sidewall, Steep Angle) to contain material on inclines.
Support web between cleats and sidewalls (Steep Angle only).
Rubber cement for bonding cleats and sidewalls. Made in-house or bought out.
Curing agent for solution. Always bought out. Dosed at ~10% of solution weight.
Shorthand like EP-1000/5 — fabric type EP, total breaking strength 1000 N/mm, 5 plies.
— areal density of a fabric layer. Drives fabric weight.
Density of a rubber compound (g/cm³). Drives rubber-layer weight.
Total Belt Thickness in mm = carcass thickness + top cover + bottom cover (+ breaker thicknesses where present).
Carcass Thickness in mm — the thickness of the fabric plies stack.
Within HR, the polymer family splits. HR and SHR are SBR-based; UHR and SUHR are EPDM-based. Their compatible skims differ. This is why the
match table allows both family-level (the common case) and compound-level (the unique case) rules — see §6.1.
A reference table (compounds, fabrics, suppliers, freight zones, …) that supplies rates and specifications to the calculation engine.
Sum of all rubber + fabric layer costs for one belt line (covers, fabric, skim, breakers, cleats, sidewalls, blinker, solution, hardener as declared by the belt type).
Material cost + Cost of Production + Packing + Freight. The total amount it costs
to produce and deliver the belt order.
Total belt cost ÷ Total length. The break-even rate per running
. Quoting below this loses money on the order.
Gross Profit percentage — margin added on top of cost to arrive at the quoted price.
Selling price = Total belt cost + (Total belt cost × GP%). GP applied to the full cost stack.
Selling price = Total belt cost + (Material cost × GP%). GP applied to material cost only; production, packing and freight are passed through at cost. Always lower than Standard.
Chosen basis (Standard or VD) × (1 − Discount%). The headline price the customer sees.
a target final rate: (target − RMC) ÷ RMC. Shows the margin a target rate produces.
Frozen copy of every rate (compound prices, SG, fabric, freight, COP, GP%, discount, exchange rate) written onto a quotation line when its parent quotation is SENT.
Marker on a compound: MANUAL (typed in costing app) or RAVASCO_COMPUTED (pulled live from the
Data lives in three layers. This is the pattern that lets masters drive every calculation while sent quotations remain immutable.
Reference data: rates, specs, lookups. The foundation.
Compound, Fabric (3 levels), Supplier, Breaker, Belt Type, Edge, Reel/Packing, Freight Zone, Cost-of-Production, Customer, Currency, Standard Reference,
Edit anytime. Every change writes a history row. Archived rows remain visible on quotes that already used them.
(one row per belt), per-line Rate Snapshot block (frozen on send)
Edit while DRAFT/PRICED. Locked once SENT. Revision creates a new version.
History: revisions, price changes, PO outcomes.
Quotation Revision, Compound Price History, Costing Config History
Append-only. Records are not deleted; corrections appear as new rows.
The hierarchy a user follows from enquiry to order:
customer's enquiry email, ref number, special note)
Quote No., date, status, currency, exchange rate)
Belt 1 — one belt = one line = one costing)
│ ├─ Belt specification (type, width, rating, covers, skim,
│ ├─ Standard price + VD price + discount + final price
Order Rate (₹/m), PO Status, PO Rate (₹/m), Agent, Actual GP %

A belt costing can also exist standalone (quick what-if pricing) and be promoted into a quotation when the customer commits. The HRS sheet is already this model, flattened into 108 columns: each row is a
, the Quote No. groups them into a Quotation, the Revised-
4 columns are versioned revisions, and PO Status / Order Rate / Agent are the post-quote fields.
End-to-end flow. Each step names the writer (table updated) and the readers (tables consulted).
Customer (defaults: GP%, currency, destination, discount tier)
Belt Type, Belt Rating, Compound (filtered by role), Edge, Width,
Compound, Belt Rating + Fabric, Breaker, Edge, Freight Zone, Cost-of-Production
Sales picks Standard or VD; sets discount; final price emerges
Send to customer — rate snapshot taken
Current phase: HTML prototype with sql.js (SQLite/WASM) +
. Single static page, no server. Masters bundled as JS files. Validates the design against the corrected Excel.
+ Prisma if Ravasco-stack alignment is preferred). Reversible until Phase 3.
Formulations App. Once linked, compound price and SG come live from the approved formulation.
flips to RAVASCO_COMPUTED; those fields become read-only.
CorrectedMultiplyBeltCosting.xlsx is the QA oracle. Any implementation must match it to the rupee on Multiply-with-Breaker before any other belt type is built. Reproduction numbers in §16.
Each master is documented below with a uniform What / Why / How / Where / When / Edge-cases structure, plus a precise field schema. The master inventory is listed first so it can be read as an index.
Every rubber compound: covers, skims, sidewall, cleat, blinker, solution, hardener.
Every per-kg rate in the cost calculation
Defines which skim is recommended for which cover (family-level or compound-level rules).
Fabric family fallback price and length wastage % (NN, EP, EE, EN, PP, Straight-Warp, Solid-Woven).
Carcass and Breaker length-wastage rule; Level-A fallback ₹/kg
The per-ply fabric SKU: GSM, thickness, supplier, price/kg (overrides type default).
Fabric weight + fabric cost in the engine
Shorthand like EP-1000/5 — decodes to per-ply fabric + carcass thickness + inter-ply skim thickness.
Carcass thickness, fabric resolution, display code
Filters Fabric and Breaker dropdowns by supplier
Breaker catalogue: type (Regular Fabric / Steel / Cross Rigid / Cord), GSM, thickness, price/kg, default mount.
Breaker top / bottom weight and cost
Form section visibility; engine component selection; COP lookup
Destination → rate + cost type (KG / SQMTR / RM).
Rate ₹/kg per belt type (Multiply ≈ 22, Steep Angle ≈ 30).
Cost of production = rate × total belt weight
Catalogue of allowed belt widths in metric (mm) and imperial (inch) sets.
No. of Ply dropdown (carcass and breaker)
Top-level family (Conveyor Belt, V-Belt, Transmission Belt).
Customer master with default destination, GP%, currency, discount tier.
INR (storage), USD, EUR, AED with manual exchange rate per quote.
IS 1891, ISO 14890, DIN, MSHA, … referenced on grade datasheets.

One row per rubber compound used anywhere in a belt — covers, skims, sidewall, cleat, blinker, solution, hardener, edge strip. A single compound can declare more than one role; for example, M-24 is typically valid as both Top Cover and Bottom Cover. This is the unified master that replaces the legacy Grade Master + separate skim references.

Every hardcoded ₹/kg in the legacy Excel (G47=80 cover, G53=65 fabric skim, G56=400 breaker, G65=88 breaker-2 skim, …) is replaced by a lookup against this master. Changing a compound’s price ripples to every new quotation that uses it.
Formulations App means integration is a one-step link rather than a migration.
Dropdowns on the form filter rows by role. The Top Cover dropdown shows compounds whose roles include TOP_COVER; Skim dropdown shows compounds whose roles include SKIM; etc.
The Skim dropdown additionally sorts recommended-first using the
Compatibility master, keyed by the chosen cover’s
from the row at calculation time; both fields are snapshotted onto the quotation line on SEND (rate freeze).
Formulations App, an admin can link a compound to a
flips to RAVASCO_COMPUTED, and SG + price become read-only, pulled live from the approved Compound Version.
Table compound (Layer 1 master). History of price changes lives in
(Layer 3). Quote snapshots live as snap_* columns on
When a row is added or edited
Adding a new compound: admin enters code, name, roles,
, status = ACTIVE. Row is immediately visible on relevant dropdowns.
row; in-flight DRAFTs and PRICEDs recompute live; SENT quotes remain frozen on their snapshot.
Archiving (status = ARCHIVED): row is hidden from new-quote pickers but remains visible on historic quotes. Cannot be deleted while referenced.
Pipe-delimited. One or many. See role list below.
. Display/filter only; does NOT drive cost.
GP / AR / HR / FR / OR / COLD / LRR. Drives skim matching.
e.g. SBR, EPDM, NBR, CR. Advisory for compatibility edge cases.
G_PER_CM3 property after integration; typed before.
MANUAL (standalone) / RAVASCO_COMPUTED. When RAVASCO_COMPUTED, SG + price are read-only.
Identifies whether the skim is intended for carcass fabric skim, steel breaker skim, or both.
Min elongation at break (%). Datasheet only.
For HR grades: HR=125, SHR=150, UHR=200, SUHR=250.
Indus Belts brand line for marketing/datasheet.
When this price came into effect.
Pulley-side cover (independently selectable from Top — locked with Jitesh)
flag distinguishes fabric-breaker vs steel-breaker vs both.
Optional intermediate layer between carcass and cover

Cross-use (user picks a compound for a role it does not declare): non-blocking warning — “M-24 is normally a Top Cover compound. You’re using it as Fabric Skim. Confirm this is intentional.” Choice + reason logged on the quote.

Archived compound picked accidentally: archived rows are filtered out of new-quote dropdowns. Historic quotes that already reference an archived compound continue to display correctly via the rate snapshot.
Price changed after a quote is sent: the sent quote uses its snapshot — does not re-price. A revision generates a new snapshot at the moment the revision is sent.
Compound with multiple roles: appears in every dropdown whose role is in its
] list — no duplication of rows needed.

A small link table that says, for each skim compound, which covers it is recommended for. A row targets either a whole grade family (the common case — GP skim ↔ GP cover, AR skim ↔ AR cover) or a specific cover compound (the unique case — the EPDM-based UHR skim only bonds to the EPDM-based UHR cover).
Rubber engineering: a cover and its skim must co-

and bond. You cannot bond an EPDM (UHR heat-resistant) cover to an NR/SBR general-purpose skim — different polymer families won’t cure together. The software must guide sales and engineering to the right pairing without forcing them, since the customer’s spec occasionally dictates a non-standard combination.
Open the Skim dropdown. The engine builds the recommended set: union of (skims with COMPOUND-level match where
= C) and (skims with FAMILY-level match where
Skim field starts EMPTY — no auto-fill. Cannot save the costing without choosing a skim.

If the user picks from “Other skims”: yellow warning “GP-Skim is not a recommended skim for a UHR (EPDM) cover. These may not bond well. Confirm this is intentional.” Choice + reason logged on the quote.
If no recommended skims exist for C (match table not yet populated for that family): info note — no warning when the user picks, since nothing is defined to violate.
GP / AR / HR / FR / OR / COLD / LRR.
Tie-breaker if multiple skims match. Affects sort order only — never auto-selects.
Free-form, e.g. ‘EPDM skim — only bonds to UHR/SUHR covers’.

Carcass reinforcement is modelled in three linked levels so the engine can resolve a price and a GSM regardless of how specific the user’s selection is. The three levels are: Fabric Type (the family), Fabric (a specific per-ply SKU from a supplier), and Belt Rating (the shop-floor shorthand).
NN, EP, EE, PP, EN, Straight-Warp, Solid-Woven.
Fallback ₹/kg when no specific Fabric SKU is bound (Level-A fallback).
Open-end length wastage. NN 4%, EP 3%, EE 3%, PP 5% (legacy Sheet0 Y–AA).
RM if fabrics are tracked there.
A specific fabric: a fabric type at a specific per-ply strength, from a specific supplier. This is the row that prices the carcass when chosen. Multi-supplier from v1.
type + per-ply rating + supplier short form.
Grams/m² (legacy Sheet0 col B; embedded J30).
Overrides Level-A default if set. NULL → use Type default.
Level C — Belt Rating (the user’s shortcut)

The shorthand the team actually says aloud: EP-1000/5. Encodes total breaking strength, ply count, and the implied per-ply fabric. When the user picks a Belt Rating, the engine knows: 5 plies of fabric EP-200, GSM 660, carcass thickness 6.8 mm — all by lookup, nothing typed.
= total ÷ ply. Points to a Level-B fabric row.
Total carcass thickness (legacy Sheet0 AG / K27).
(1) If a Fabric SKU is bound to the line and has
This was the explicit decision: type-level default + per-fabric override, no admin approval required to use the type default.

Catalogue of available breaker materials. A belt may have a Breaker on Top, a Breaker on Bottom, both, or neither (independent toggles). Each side has its own breaker row reference, ply count, mount (Floating or Non-Floating), and an associated skim chosen from Compound master (skim is selected once at the line level, in Conveyor Belt Details — see 5.1).
Regular Fabric / Cross Rigid Fabric / Steel Breaker / Cord Breaker.
Initial ply count for the line; user-overridable.
FLOATING / NON_FLOATING. The team’s good catch from flow_Edited.docx; affects layer ordering, not weight.
On the form, after Breaker Type + Make are chosen, GSM / Thickness / Price-per-kg are pulled from this master and displayed read-only. They cannot be edited on the form. Admins add a
row for any non-catalogued breaker; the form never has writable cost fields.
The 14 belt types. Each row declares which components the belt uses, which length rule applies, and which calculation family it belongs to. This is what drives the form’s show/hide
and what the engine consults to know which component weights/costs to compute.
→ Product Type master (Conveyor Belt, V-Belt, Transmission Belt).
BASE / BASE_BREAKER / BASE_CLEAT / SIDEWALL / FULL / BASE_PIPE.
Pipe-delimited list. Engine sums only these components.
FALSE = Pipe and Paper Reel (v2). Hidden from new-quote pickers in v1.
Full 14-row table with each type’s family and components is in §7 — The Belt Type Matrix.
The physical edge finish. Drives the width wastage added to the nominal belt width for material weight calculation. Fixes the legacy bug where the Multiply sheet hardcoded +30 mm in formula B47 = ((B
Combined master for both reel types (how the belt is rolled — Single Roll, Swing Roll, Cassette, Circular) and packing types (HDPE Packing, Wooden Crate, Steel Crate). A single row carries
flag distinguishing the two, and a
REEL / PACKING. Drives where the row appears in the form.
Per-destination freight rate. Replaces the legacy pattern of hardcoded freight in each belt-type sheet (B38/B43/B54) and the silent-zero failure when destination text was misspelled.
Rate in the unit defined by
KG: rate × belt weight. SQMTR: rate ×
× 1 m. RM: rate × total length (flat per
Production overhead rate ₹/kg, keyed by belt type (locked with Jitesh — rate varies by belt type, e.g. Multiply ≈ 22, Steep Angle ≈ 30). Was hardcoded per belt-type sheet in legacy.
₹/kg production expense for this belt type.
Plant-level variation can be added later by extending the key to (
Catalogue of allowed widths. Two independent sets distinguished by

: metric (mm, 500–2400 in 50 mm intervals typical) and imperial (inches). The Width dropdown shows whichever set matches the user’s Unit System preference. Custom widths go through the Request-Custom-Width approval workflow (see §11 edge cases).
METRIC / IMPERIAL — partitions the two sets.
Simple lookup: values 1 to 8. Bound to the No. of Ply dropdown (carcass) and the Breaker Ply Count dropdown. One column (value INTEGER).

Top-level family. Filters the Belt Type dropdown so a user choosing “Conveyor Belt” sees only conveyor variants. Three rows in v1: CB (Conveyor Belt), VB (V-Belt), TB (Transmission Belt). V1 wires only CB; VB/TB exist as placeholders.
One row per customer. Stores defaults that pre-fill quotation header fields. All pre-fills remain editable per quote.
Whether freight is rolled into rate or billed separately.
A / B / C — default discount tier.

INR is the storage currency for every quote. Non-INR currencies (USD, EUR, AED) are display-only conversions, using a per-quote manual exchange rate entered by the user (no master default — prevents stale-rate errors).
Indicative; the per-quote rate is entered manually.
Catalogue of standards referenced on grade datasheets and quotations: IS 1891, ISO 14890, DIN-X/Y/W, MSHA Part 14, AS, SANS, RMA. Used by the Compound master’s
field and by the printed datasheet. Mirrors

Every formula below is deterministic given the line state. Every rate it consumes comes from a master row. No rate is ever typed inside a formula. The engine is pure — it takes a state snapshot in, returns a result snapshot out, and never touches the DOM or the database directly.
Every component weight reduces to one of these two forms. The rest of the engine is composition.
Solid rubber layer (cover, skim, breaker skim, sidewall, cleat compound, blinker)
Fabric / woven layer (carcass fabric, breaker fabric — priced by GSM)
fixes audit bug C8 — wastage only for cut edge)
fixes audit bug X2 — one rule, driven by belt type)
# NN 4 %, EP 3 %, EE 3 %, PP 5 % from Fabric Type master
# Replaces hardcoded +3 in legacy, +4 in HRS dealer sheet.
). That ensures the customer is charged for what they ordered, while the engine internally accounts for the wastage in material weight and cost.
Component 1 — Top & Bottom Covers (independently selectable compounds — locked)
Top and Bottom may use DIFFERENT compounds and DIFFERENT SGs.
Weights are computed split — NEVER averaged or collapsed to a single SG.
Rough Top / Wavy Top = same engine, simply a larger
(the texture is extra cover rubber — locked with Jitesh).
Component 3 — Carcass Skim (remainder method — preserved from validated Excel)
# Approximate the no-breaker sandwich at the cover SG, then subtract
# cover and fabric weights. The leftover is the skim rubber between plies.
than the sandwich). WARN the user prominently. Do not silently clamp.
: surface the impossibility, allow save with a
hard warning so the input mistake gets fixed at review time.

” used in the no-breaker sandwich approximation is asymmetric when Top and Bottom use different compounds with different SGs. The validated Excel uses one cover SG (legacy D44 = 1.18). Locked decision LD-1 (§15): use top_compound.SG. The skim weight is surfaced in the breakdown so the assumption is visible.
Component 4 — Breakers (Top and/or Bottom — independent toggles)
# Computed per side that is enabled. Both sides may differ in type,
# supplier, GSM, thickness, ply count, mount, and price.
# The breaker carries its own skim layer. Skim compound is the one
# selected at line level (in Conveyor Belt Details), not duplicated here.
Fabric breakers (Regular Fabric, Cross Rigid Fabric) expect
A mismatch produces a soft warning on the cost breakdown.
Mount (FLOATING vs NON_FLOATING) affects layer ordering in the belt
but NOT weight. Same weight, different bond.
# Two methods exist in the source sheets; we pick one. Fixes audit bug C3.
# Steep Angle — count × weight-per-cleat with wastage:
Component 7 — Blinkers (Steep Angle only) — fixes audit bug C5
= TRUE: # the Yes/No toggle now actually works
Legacy Steep Angle B36 (Blinkers Yes/No) was cosmetic — B101/B115/B131
added blinker cost regardless. In v1 the toggle drives the engine.
# covers + fabric + skim + breakers + cleat + sidewall + blinker + solution + hardener
# NOTE: Expenses appear ONLY here as
(fixes audit bug C1 — always divide by length)
This is the absolute minimum the company can quote per running
Quoting below this loses money on the order.
B86 = B74 # WRONG — total, not per-
C92 = C91/B26 # WRONG — divides by 'Pieces', not length
Two pricing bases are computed for every line and shown side by side. Sales picks one (per line), applies discount, and the final price is the headline number on the quote.
GP is applied to the FULL cost stack (material + production + packing + freight).
Safe floor for the company. Default basis.
Always lower than Standard (assuming non-material costs > 0).
Used for competitive bids where the customer needs an aggressive number.
Discount is applied AFTER GP. For one-off negotiated reductions
a target final rate, system shows implied margin
(fixes legacy bug C6 which silently returned GP% in the price field):
Selling prices are shown exactly as computed. The legacy
price, 5) in the HRS dealer sheet is removed. Locked with Jitesh.
INR is the storage currency. Exchange rate is entered manually per quote
The Exchange Rate field is hidden when Currency = INR.
sales benchmark rates across belts of different widths.
# Once a PO is received and Order Rate is filled:
Negative value = sold below RMC (alarm state).
Used in win-loss and margin retention reports.
The corrected Multiply-with-Breaker workbook (CorrectedMultiplyBeltCosting.xlsx) reproduces the legacy Excel to the rupee on the validated scenario:
The Phase-1 software engine must match these numbers for the same inputs. This is the gate that opens Phase 2 (other belt types).

All 14 belt types in one table. Each row tells the engine and the form which components to include and which length rule to apply. v1 ships the 12 types whose status is TRUE; Pipe and Paper Reel are v2.
Same as #7 (often punched holes — open Q)
BASE + cleats + sidewalls + blinkers + solution + hardener

This section is the authoritative field-by-field reference for the costing form. The structure preserves the team’s UI work (which is good) and applies the architectural corrections from the master catalogue. Sections appear on the form in the order shown.
Customer’s own reference (from their email). User-entered. Format not enforced.
System-generated. Exact format deferred to the team (locked: format not yet decided).
Line number within a Quotation, starting 0001 and incrementing per belt added. Padded 4 digits.
. Starts at 1. Increments on revision (new versioned row, previous version preserved).
Today, set when quote first created.
Selecting pre-fills default destination, currency, GP%, freight-inclusion preference, discount tier. All pre-fills remain editable.
Customer-specific instruction per enquiry (e.g. ‘FR grade only’, ‘deliver in 2
Top-level family. Filters Belt Type dropdown. CB / VB / TB.
Dropdown (Belt Type master, filtered by Product Type)
Drives form section visibility (breaker/cleat/sidewall/blinker), length rule, COP rate. The single most important choice.
User preference. Display only — storage is always metric. Saved per user, not per quote.
Metric set 500–2400 mm by 50 mm; imperial
inches. Custom widths → Request-Custom-Width admin workflow (line stays DRAFT until resolved).
User picks full code (e.g. EP-1000/5). Filter chips above the dropdown narrow by fabric family (All / NN / EP / EE / EN / PP). Chip state is UI-only, never persisted.
. NEVER a user input — derived. Updates live on Belt Rating change.
, suffixed N/mm. NEVER a user input.
User-selected per client requirement. Independent of Belt Rating’s nominal ply (Belt Rating defines per-ply rating; ply count is the user’s choice).
Decimal, half-mm steps allowed. Typical 2–10 mm.
Decimal, half-mm steps allowed. Typical 1–6 mm. Independent of Top — no enforced ≤ rule.
UI-only narrowing for the three compound dropdowns below. Never persisted.
Dropdown (Compound master, role contains TOP_COVER, filtered by chip)
User-selected. Defaults same as previous belt’s top cover, but always editable.
Dropdown (Compound master, role contains BOTTOM_COVER, filtered by chip)
Defaults to Top Cover Compound on initial load (a common pattern), then independent.
Dropdown (Compound master, role contains SKIM, recommended-first via §5.2)
Recommended skims for the chosen Top Cover’s grade family appear first. Picking a non-recommended skim shows a warning, never a block.
. Drives the +30 / +0 mm width wastage. Replaces hardcoded +30 in legacy.
8.4 Breaker Details block (shown when Belt Type supports breaker)
= FALSE. When visible, has a master toggle plus two independent sub-blocks (Top and Bottom).
Master toggle. No = hide both sub-blocks AND switch the belt-type internal flag to the non-breaker variant for COP lookup.
Bottom sub-block enable. Bottom-only is a common pattern for impact-prone top-feed belts.
Regular Fabric / Cross Rigid Fabric / Steel / Cord.
Dropdown (Supplier master, filtered to suppliers of the chosen Breaker Type)
Live filter — narrows supplier list to those who supply this breaker type.
Loop circumference. +3 m splice added by engine.
Pre-filled from customer’s default; editable per quote.
Yes = freight rolled into quoted rate. No = billed separately.
Belt Type confirmed (read-only echo). Packing Type dropdown (Reel/Packing master, filtered to
= PACKING). Reel Type dropdown is shown separately when applicable (

All overrides are blank by default, meaning the engine uses master-sourced values. When filled, the override replaces the master value at the step where it is first used, and the affected weight/cost line shows an “Overridden” badge in the breakdown.
Weight-driving (LD-2 §15). Override flows into carcass thickness in the skim remainder.
Top Cover compound SG used in skim remainder
Sandwich weight only; cover and bottom-cover weights still use their compounds’ own SGs.
Override paired with Top / Bottom selector (LD-4 §15). Default Top; user can switch or apply both.
Default = customer default, else INR. If INR, Exchange Rate field is hidden.
Numeric, user-entered, required if Currency ≠ INR
No master default. ‘₹ per 1 {currency}’ label, e.g. 83.50 ₹/USD.
Range 0–80. Above 50 % shows a soft warning.
Sales picks per line. Both rates always shown side-by-side regardless of choice.
(m) — with the applied wastage rule named (e.g. ‘+3 % open-end’ or ‘+3 m splice
Specific Gravity used for sandwich (with override badge if applicable)
Per-meter weight breakdown: Fabric, Cover (Top + Bottom split), Skim, Breaker (each side split if both present). Each on its own row.
(kg/m) — MANUAL, filled post-production by QC for variance reporting (team’s good idea, adopted)
Fabric Cost/m, Cover Cost/m (Top + Bottom split), Skim Cost/m, Breaker Cost/m (top + bottom split)
Standard Rate/m (CD) — GP on full cost
VD Rate/m — GP on material only
Per-MM Running Price (= Final Rate/m ÷ width mm)
Foreign currency rate (when currency ≠ INR) = Final Rate INR/m ÷ exchange rate
Total RMC Amount, Total Quotation Amount, Total RMC VD, Total Quotation VD (all = per-
× Total Length, with Discount applied to Quotation totals)
8.12 Revisions & Order Status block (post-quote tracking, all optional)
Revisions list — each entry: Input (what changed) + Rate (the new rate). Unlimited revisions; the printed quote shows the last four.
Order Rate (₹/m) — rate at which the order was actually procured. Filled when PO arrives.
Actual GP % Received — auto-computed when Order Rate is filled: (Order Rate − RMC) ÷ RMC × 100.
PO Status — Pending / Received / Lost / No PO.
PO Rate (₹/m) — final rate on customer’s purchase order. Usually = Order Rate; may differ.
A quotation line moves through the following states. Each transition has a precise trigger and a precise consequence.
Sales is still entering inputs. Required fields may be incomplete.
All required inputs are filled; calculation is complete; in internal review before sending.
Quote has been emailed to the customer. Rate freeze fires HERE.
Customer asked for changes. A new
row is created (incremented version), copying all fields from the SENT version. New snapshot is taken. Previous version is preserved.
Customer issued a PO. Order Rate filled. Actual GP% computed.
Customer chose competitor or dropped enquiry.
the thing that fixes the legacy danger)

, every quotation row references Sheet0 via VLOOKUP. Editing a fabric price on Sheet0 silently changes every past quote’s numbers. There is no way to know what a quote looked like when it was sent, and no audit trail. This is the single most damaging legacy
In v1, the moment status transitions to SENT, the system writes a per-line snapshot of every rate that drove the calculation:
Exchange Rate entered on the quote (if non-INR)
After SENT, the calculation engine uses only these snapshot values — never live master prices. A change to Compound master tomorrow does not move the sent quote by a single rupee.
A revision does not overwrite the sent line. It produces a new versioned line that carries its own fresh snapshot.
Sales opens the sent line and selects ‘Revise’.
row is created with version = previous + 1, all fields copied. Status = DRAFT (revising).
Sales edits whatever changed — typically rate, GP%, discount, but specifications too if customer changed their mind.
Engine recomputes live from current masters.
On Send (of the revision), a new snapshot is taken. State moves to SENT-as-Revised. Both versions remain visible in the line’s history.
A Quotation Revision row is also written, with
The printed quotation shows the most recent four revisions by default (matching the legacy HRS convention). The full revision history is

When a sent quote is revised, the customer-facing PDF can be regenerated. The PDF should display ‘Revision N’ in the header, the date of the revision, and a short summary of what changed (auto-populated from the revision row’s ‘input’ field).
All post-quote fields are optional. A quote is never blocked for lacking them. They are filled when the customer responds or the order arrives:
Order Rate ₹/m — the rate at which the customer actually placed the order.
PO Status — Pending / Received / Lost / No PO.
PO Rate — final rate on the customer’s PO document.
Actual GP % Received — auto-computed from Order Rate and RMC.
Actual Belt Weight/m — auto-computed from production QC measurement, used in freight variance and margin review.
This section walks five real scenarios from input to

quotation, with every intermediate number stated. Each case is selected to exercise a different part of the engine and the lifecycle — together they cover the surface area a Phase-1/2 implementation must satisfy.
10.1 UC-1 — Multiply with Breaker (the QA oracle)

Purpose: this is the reproduction test. The new engine, given the same inputs the legacy Multiply Excel sheet was run on, must match the corrected Excel’s figures to the rupee. If UC-1 doesn’t pass, no other belt type is built.
Drawn from CorrectedMultiplyBeltCosting.xlsx → Multiply with Breaker tab. All values are user inputs or master-driven (none are typed inside formulas).
Each step shows the canonical formula from §6 and the substituted value. All weights in kg, all lengths in m, all rates in ₹/kg unless noted.
Step 5 — Belt-weight-no-breaker (intermediate, used by skim remainder)
Note: the original Excel computes carcass thickness explicitly from the fabric master.
For oracle parity, follow the same chain:
then nominal belt weight = (top + carcass + bottom volumetric).
top cover’s SG for the rubber volume share — locked decision LD-1 §15.)
Step 7 — Breaker 1 and Breaker 2 (fabric + skim each)
Same shape as fabric and skim, using breaker-master GSM and Breaker compound SG/rates.
Step 8 — Total belt weight (all components summed) = 22,868.76 kg ← oracle
If any of these four figures deviates from the oracle by ≥ ₹1, UC-1 fails. Source: 07-corrected-excel-spec.md → Validation table.
10.2 UC-2 — Steep Angle (full family — every optional component active)
Purpose: prove the engine handles a belt that declares every optional component in 03-belt-type-matrix.md — cleats, sidewalls, blinkers, solution, hardener — without any hardcoded branches.
Top Cover, Bottom Cover — standard cover layers.
Cleats — cleat compound (role = CLEAT), with 10% wastage on cleat rubber per audit S3.
Sidewalls — sidewall compound (role = SIDEWALL), with 10% wastage.
Blinkers — blinker compound (role = BLINKER); this is the toggle that fixes bug C5.
Solution — solution compound (role = SOLUTION); applied to cleat + sidewall area.
Hardener — at 10% of solution weight (parameter on Belt Type, per audit S3).
If Blinkers = No on the form,
must both be zero. (Old Excel ignored this toggle — bug C5.)
Free lateral space must derive cleanly from width, sidewall base, and cleat layout — no orphan cell reference (old Excel had B26 dangling — bug C4).
Cleat compound is calculated once, not twice with the second silently overriding (old Excel — bug C3).
= width/1000 (no +30 mm) — wastage is parametric, not sheet-hardcoded (bug C8).

Run the corrected-Excel Steep-Angle equivalent (to be produced once UC-1 is anchored) with: Blinkers=Yes vs Blinkers=No → blinker cost differs by exactly the blinker compound contribution and nothing else. Toggle Edge between Cut and
→ only the width wastage line changes.
10.3 UC-3 — Quotation revision (rate freeze in action)
Purpose: prove that a sent quotation never moves when masters move, and that revisions are versioned, not overwrites.

Day 1, 10:00 — Sales creates Quotation QTN/26-27/0906-0042 for customer Tata Steel. One belt line: Multiply EP-800/4, 1000 mm × 100 m. RMC = ₹2,140.00/m. GP% = 30%. Standard = ₹2,782.00/m. Quotation status = DRAFT.

Day 1, 14:00 — Sales clicks SEND. The system writes the rate snapshot: every master rate (cover ₹/kg, fabric ₹/kg, skim ₹/kg, breaker ₹/kg, COP ₹/kg, freight ₹/kg, packing ₹/m) is copied into snapshot_* columns on the quotation line. Status = SENT, version = 1.

Day 7 — Admin updates M-24 cover rate in the Compound Master from ₹80 to ₹85/kg (a master change). All new quotes from now use ₹85; the cover-rate price-history table records the change with user, timestamp, old, new.
Day 10 — Sales reopens QTN/26-27/0906-0042. The quote displays ₹80 for the cover rate (from snapshot), RMC unchanged at ₹2,140.00/m, Standard ₹2,782.00/m. Nothing has moved.

Day 12 — Customer asks for a revised offer at the lower price. Sales clicks REVISE. The system creates Quotation Line version 2 by cloning version 1, recomputes against current masters (₹85 cover now), and produces RMC = ₹2,168.00/m at GP% 30 → Standard = ₹2,818.40/m. Sales adjusts GP% to 27% to land the target → Standard = ₹2,753.36/m. Status of v2 = SENT, version = 2; version 1 remains visible and unchanged.
Opening v1 always shows the v1 numbers — no matter how many master changes have happened since.
v1 and v2 are both visible on the same quotation, with their own snapshots and timestamps.
The Quotation PDF for v1 and v2 are independently regenerable from their own snapshot.
Purpose: prove that storage stays in INR and USD is purely a display + freeze concern at quote level.
Currency selected on Quotation = USD. The exchange rate is read from the Currency / Exchange-Rate Master at SEND time: 1 USD = ₹83.20.
in INR. The engine produces Standard ₹/m and VD ₹/m as usual.
Display layer divides by 83.20 at the UI edge for the customer-facing PDF; the cost sheet stays INR for internal teams.
is part of the rate snapshot. Reopening the quote three months later, with USD now at ₹86.00, still shows the original USD figures because the snapshot rate is fixed.
Purpose: handle a width not present in the Width Master without breaking the masters-as-single-source-of-truth principle.
Sales begins a quote and the width dropdown does not contain the requested 1350 mm. Sales clicks ‘Request New Width’.
System creates a Width-Request record (status = PENDING_ADMIN) capturing the requested value, the customer, and the requesting user.

Sales is still able to compose the quote as a Provisional quote — every rate is computed normally but the quote header carries a banner ‘Provisional — width 1350 mm pending Admin approval’ and the SEND button is disabled.

Admin reviews the width request, adds 1350 mm to the Width Master (or rejects). On approval, the Provisional flag clears and SEND becomes available; the recorded quote rebinds to the now-valid master row.
Every edge case below has a defined
. The principle is the same in all of them: never silently zero. The legacy Excel’s habit of wrapping master lookups in
..., 0) caused under-quoting; the new system either succeeds with a real number or hard-blocks the quotation with a visible reason.
Trigger: any rate or spec referenced by the calculation is missing from the master (e.g., a compound row is deleted while a quote is in DRAFT, or a fabric grade was never seeded).
: hard-block. The form surfaces a precise error — ‘Compound code M-24 not found in Compound Master’ — and the SEND button is disabled. The calculation engine raises a typed exception (
) that the UI maps to a user-fixable action: ‘Add the master row or change the selection’.
Rationale: 01-audit-findings.md ‘Silent Failures’ — the old Main sheet returned 0 for missing AJ5/AL5/AM5/BB5; the belt was then under-costed silently. This must not be possible in the new system.
Trigger: user picks a top cover with grade family HR and a skim whose family is GP (the bond chemistry doesn’t match).

: the dropdown lists recommended skims (matching family) at the top with a green indicator; non-matching skims are still selectable but show an amber warning ‘Skim grade family does not match top cover — bonding risk’. The selection is logged on the quotation line for audit. The quote is not blocked — the warning is advisory, because there are legitimate edge cases.
Trigger: a deliberately mis-spec’d belt where covers + carcass volume already exceed the nominal belt volume, leaving negative space for skim.
< 0 before persisting and hard-blocks with the error ‘Skim weight resolved to a negative value — check cover thicknesses and fabric
. The legacy Excel silently produced a negative
number; the new system does not allow it to flow into the total.
11.4 Archived compound on an existing quote
Trigger: an admin archives compound code M-24 (status=ARCHIVED). An old quote in DRAFT still references it.
: archived compounds vanish from new-quote pickers but remain readable on existing records. The DRAFT quote can still be edited and SENT — the rate is whatever the master holds
today; on SEND the rate is snapshotted as usual. The Compound Master keeps history; the relationship is by master ID, not by code-string.
Trigger: the requested belt width does not exist as a row in the Width Master.

: the Request-with-Provisional-Quote workflow runs (see UC-5 §10.5). The user can compose and view the quote but cannot SEND it until Admin approves the new width into the Width Master. This protects the principle that every dropdown value is a master row — no free-text widths leaking into stored quotes.
Trigger: a user selects INR as the display currency on a domestic quote.
: identity transformation. The exchange-rate row for INR is fixed at 1.0; storage and display are both INR. The snapshot still captures
= 1.0 to keep the snapshot schema uniform.
11.7 Endless belt with very short total length
Trigger: an endless belt of 2 m total length, with the locked 3 m flat splice allowance.

+ 3 = 5 m. The 3 m allowance is a wastage of 150% on a 2 m belt — economically unusable but mathematically defined. The system flags ‘Short endless belt — splice allowance dominates length cost’ as a soft warning but does not block. This makes the legacy bug (audit S3 — flat 3 m allowance is wrong for short belts) visible to the user instead of hiding it.
11.8 Discount drops final price below RMC
a discount large enough that the final price per meter is less than the per-meter raw material cost (i.e., loss-making sale).

: the form shows ‘Implied GP% = negative N%’ in red. The quote is not blocked automatically — pricing-below-cost is a deliberate business choice that requires a separate approval discussion (see open question OQ-3). The system makes the loss visible and unmissable; whether it requires an approval gate is the permissions question still under discussion with Jitesh.
Trigger: some freight rows in HRSMASTERSHEETC2526HRSNEW are per-kg, others are per-meter, and a few legacy rows have neither populated cleanly.
(PER_KG / PER_METER / FLAT_PER_QUOTE). The engine reads basis and applies accordingly: per-kg → rate ×
; flat → rate added once. Old free-text freight columns are excluded from seed import; the team curates the freight master fresh.
Trigger: user picks Cut Edge, computes, then switches to
recalculates immediately from the Edge Master’s
). All downstream weights and costs ripple through the live breakdown. This is the canonical fix for legacy bug C8 — wastage is no longer hardcoded per sheet.
Trigger: a single quotation holds two belt lines, one priced in INR and one in USD.

: currency is a quote-header attribute, not per-line. Mixed currencies inside one quote are not supported in v1 — the form enforces a single currency at header level. A multi-currency need is a v2 feature.
Every bug from the audit becomes an acceptance criterion. Phase 1 cannot be declared complete until each
below has a passing test. This section is the bridge between the audit findings document and the test pack.
Endless belt RMC compared total cost (not per-meter) against custom price — Reverse GP% meaningless. C92 divided by B26 (=10 pieces) not B23 (length), so per-meter price was wrong by ~3×.
Conv. Belt sheet, B86 / C86 / C92
, always. One canonical formula in the engine — no per-sheet variation.
Total Length cell B26 was a hardcoded ‘10’ (pieces) instead of length × pieces. Packing cost = ₹4 × 10 = ₹40 for a 291.9 m belt — 29× under-count.
is always pieces × length-per-piece on the form, and packing =
(or × pieces depending on packing basis). Validated by UC-1 and the Endless equivalent.
Cleat compound had two rows; only the second was used. User edits to row 73 were silently ignored.
Chevron Conveyor Belt sheet, rows 73 and 74
Each belt component is declared once. The Cleat compound has exactly one row in the per-belt costing — driven by the Belt Type Master’s
Free Lateral Space referenced empty cell B26 (cleat was removed). Worked by accident.
Layout formulas reference declared component rows only. No orphan cells. Field-level form spec (§8) enforces this.
Steep Angle ‘Blinkers Yes/No’ toggle did nothing — blinker cost was always included.
Steep Angle Conveyor Belt sheet, B36 → B101/B115/B131
Belt Type Master includes optional component flags. UI toggle gates the calculation — ‘Blinkers=No’ →
= 0. Acceptance: UC-2 toggles this and observes a clean delta.
Dealer Mainsheet Revised Quotation Rate formula returned GP% (a percentage) when revised rate ≥ 25, not a price.
Revisions are versioned records (see §9). No mode-flag formula. The Revised Rate column is a price; the GP% column is a percentage; they don’t share a cell.
Fabric Style lookup fell through silently above strength range (e.g., EP 800/5) → GSM = 0 → fabric cost = 0.
Belt Rating Master carries every valid (
, strength, plies) combination explicitly. A missing combination hard-blocks the quote (per §11.1) — never zeros.
Width wastage (+30 mm) was applied unconditionally in costing-template sheets, even when the belt was
These are not arithmetic errors but architectural debt. Each one is closed by moving the value out of formulas and into a master row, where it has a single owner, a price history, and an audit trail.

Per-kg rates baked into 30+ formula cells across 6 belt-type sheets (cover ₹80/125, fabric ₹240, fabric skim ₹65/85, breaker ₹400/550, cleat ₹150, sidewall ₹150, solution ₹1000, hardener ₹750, blinker ₹150)
field per row); zero rates inside any formula.
Specific gravities (cover 1.18, breaker skim 1.22/1.25, sidewall 1.25) hardcoded in D-column cells across sheets
Wastage rules: 3% length open-end, 3 m flat endless, 4 m dealer-sheet endless, 10% cleat/sidewall, 10% hardener, 30 mm cut-edge width
Cost of Production rate (₹22–30/kg) hardcoded per sheet
Freight rate hardcoded per costing-template sheet
Freight Master with destination → rate lookup (the dealer sheet already had this
Reel/Packing/Edge types as free-text strings (typos silently broke lookups)
flag (REEL/PACKING); Edge Master; controlled dropdowns only.
Fabric Skim rate (₹65) sat in G53 with no compound name attached — no dropdown existed for it
Fabric Skim is a first-class Compound Master row with role = SKIM and
= Fabric. The dropdown exists on the form (per §8).
The same calculation was done differently across belt-type sheets. The new engine collapses these to a single canonical path.
RMC denominator differed (per-meter in Multiply, total in Endless, etc.)
Length-wastage rule differed (3% open-end vs flat 3 m endless vs flat 4 m in dealer sheet)
(PERCENT / FLAT_METERS) and the value. Engine applies according to basis.
Cover SG = 1.18 in some sheets, 1.25 in others — for the same compound family
SG is always read from the Compound Master row, not from a sheet-local cell.
Same cell address (e.g., I33) held different compound names across sheets — choice travelled by cell reference, not master ID
Compound selection is a master-ID foreign key on the quotation line. Cell addresses are an implementation detail of the legacy sheet only.
Cleat handling was different per sheet — some had it, some had stub cells, some had a confusing double row
] declaration is the truth. Form sections render only declared components.
Wherever the legacy Main sheet used
..., 0), the new system raises a typed
exception and hard-blocks the quote. Specifically:
AJ5 carcass thickness — fabric not in master → was 0, now blocks.
AL5 / AM5 / AN5 / AV5 — grade not in master → were 0, now block.
AY5 length factor — fabric type not in master → was 0, now blocks.
BS5 freight — state spelling wrong → was 0, now controlled dropdown so this is impossible.
BB5 GSM — fabric style mismatch → was 0, now blocks.
This is the single most important
change: the new system refuses to under-quote. Sales cannot accidentally send a quote at half price because a master row was missing.

The platform targets a PostgreSQL relational database with a strict three-layer architecture: Master, Transaction, and Audit. This section gives the canonical table inventory and the field-level shape of the core tables. The shape mirrors the locked master architecture (§5) and the
-Costing-DB-Schema document; where the two diverge, the locked architecture wins.
Every table belongs to exactly one of three layers, and the layer determines the allowed mutation pattern and the audit obligation.
Reference rates, specs, and lookup values. Compound, fabric, breaker, edge, reel/packing, freight, cost-of-production, belt type, width, ply, customer, currency.
CRUD by Admin only. Archive (status flag), never hard-delete.
Every price/spec change writes a price-history row with user, timestamp, old value, new value.
The working data: quotations and their belt lines. Each line references master IDs and carries a frozen snapshot on SEND.
INSERT on draft. UPDATE allowed while DRAFT. Read-only once SENT.
Every state transition writes a row in
Price history, quotation event log, revision lineage, login events. The history layer.
Every change to a compound’s price/kg or SG.
Family-level and per-compound match rules between covers and skims.
Top level (NN, EP, EE, EN, PP) with type-level defaults.
Breaker fabric/steel rows with GSM, ply count, price/kg.
Customer record with destination and agent.
Currency codes with exchange rate to INR.
Quotation header (ID QTN/{FY}/{DDMM}-{NNNN}) — currency, terms, header-level.
One belt costing per row, with snapshot_* columns set on SEND.
Versioned revisions; each is a clone of a prior
State transitions (DRAFT→SENT, etc.) with actor, timestamp.
Login users; permissions matrix per Jitesh’s separate discussion.
The Compound table is the foundation. Every rubber consumption — covers, skim, cleat, sidewall, blinker, solution, hardener, edge — reads from one row in this table.
-- GP / AR / HR / FR / OR / COLD / LRR
is one belt costing. The snapshot_* columns are the rate-freeze contract: they are null while DRAFT, populated atomically on SEND, immutable thereafter.
-- COMPUTED (recomputed live until SEND; snapshot reflects SEND-time values)
-- SNAPSHOT — populated on SEND, never overwritten thereafter
7,4) representing the number (e.g., 35.0000 = 35%), not the fraction.
All weights kg, all lengths m, all thicknesses mm. Imperial display is a UI concern only — never stored.
Unique index on compound(code) — code is the human key.
GIN index on compound(roles) — for the role filter on dropdowns.
Trigger: any UPDATE to a SENT
that touches a snap_* column → raise exception. Snapshots are immutable.
When a compound is linked to a
Formulations App compound, the link sets:
become read-only in the costing app UI — they are pulled from
continue to display their snapshot — they do not re-price. Only new quotes use the

The build is sequenced so the team has something testable early and validates correctness before piling on breadth. The guiding rule, from 06-phasing-plan.md, is: masters before engine; engine before breadth; breadth before packaging; packaging before polish; integration last. Belt type #2 is not started until belt type #1 reproduces the corrected Excel to the rupee.
Nothing computes without masters. Phase 0 is bedrock — every master built with full CRUD, audit trail, and price history.
Login + users (basic; full role matrix deferred to Jitesh’s permissions discussion — leave seams).
Compound master with all fields per §13.3, including
Fabric Type, Fabric Per-Ply, Belt Rating, Fabric Supplier, Breaker.
Edge, Reel/Packing, Freight Zone, Cost-of-Production (per belt type), Belt Type (
Width, No-of-Ply, Product Type, Customer, Currency / Exchange-Rate.
Admin can create / edit / archive every master.
A price change writes a history row (user, timestamp, old, new).
Archived items disappear from new-quote pickers but remain readable on existing records.
14.2 Phase 1 — Engine + ONE belt type (Multiply with Breaker)
The vertical slice. Build the calculation engine and the form for the one belt type already validated in the corrected Excel.
The calculation engine: covers, fabric, skim-by-remainder, breakers, totals, RMC, pricing ladder.
Belt input form for Multiply-with-Breaker, with all field controls per §8.
Component dropdowns: role-filtered, top/bottom cover independently selectable, fabric-skim as a first-class dropdown.
Skim-cover compatibility: recommended skims listed first, mismatch warning rendered.
Live cost breakdown with Standard + VD prices side by side.
Hard-validation: missing master row → visible block, never silent zero.
Same inputs as the corrected Excel reproduce: weight 22,868.76 kg, total cost ₹3,339,183.16, RMC ₹3,339.18/m, Standard ₹4,507.90/m, VD ₹4,286.39/m. (UC-1 in §10.)
Bug fixes C1, C2, C7, C8 demonstrably pass.
This is the correctness gate. Phase 2 does not start until Phase 1 is signed off.
14.3 Phase 2 — The other 11 v1 belt types
Each is ‘declare components, reuse engine’. No new math except the family-specific bits already documented in 04-calculation-engine.md.
Multi-Ply (no breaker), Bag Diverter, Bucket Elevator, Straight Warp — pure base /
Rough Top, Wavy Top — base with thicker top cover.
Endless, Jointless — base with the +3 m splice rule (fixes C1 and C2).
Sidewall — base + sidewall + solution + hardener (fixes C4).
Steep Angle — the FULL family: cleats + sidewalls + blinkers + solution + hardener (fixes C5; covered by UC-2).
Each belt type reproduces its Excel sheet (for the 4 with one) or matches its declared formula (for the rest).
The form shows only that belt type’s component sections — no stray cleat / sidewall / blinker lines (closes X5).
14.4 Phase 3 — Quotation packaging and the rate freeze
Turn costings into customer quotations. This is where the lifecycle (§9) lands.
Customer → Enquiry → Quotation with multiple belt lines.
Pick Standard or VD per line → apply discount → final price.
Rate snapshot on SEND — every master rate frozen onto the line.
Revisions as versioned records (Revised-1, Revised-2 …); prior versions preserved and viewable.
State machine enforced (DRAFT → SENT → REVISED → … per §9).
Send a quote → change a compound price in masters → reopen the sent quote → numbers unchanged. (UC-3.)
Create a revision → both versions visible on the same quotation, with independent snapshots.
Quotation PDF — customer-facing, branded Indus / HRS. One PDF per enquiry with all belts as pages.
Cost sheet — internal breakdown per component, downloadable.
Won / Lost + optional PO tracking: order rate, PO status, agent, actual GP, actual belt weight/m.
Currency display for export quotes (USD as snapshot at SEND).
Generate a branded quote PDF; the PDF reflects the snapshot, not the current masters.
Mark a quote Won with an order rate; actual GP computes against the snapshot RMC.
The integration that makes prices live. Decoupled from v1 daily-use — but the seams are designed in from Phase 0.
Admin links each costing compound to a
→ RAVASCO_COMPUTED; price and SG become read-only in the costing UI.
Optionally pull fabric and other raw-material prices through the same pattern.
→ the linked compound re-prices → new belt quotes reflect it; previously SENT quotes stay frozen on their own snapshot.
14.7 v2 (after v1 is in daily use)
Pipe belt (tube geometry) + Paper Reel — the two belt types held back from v1.
Plant-level cost-of-production (currently per belt type only).
Advanced reports — win-rate, margin trends, price-list generation.
Bulk price updates; compound where-used; multi-currency at line level.
All masters with CRUD + price history
Admin sign-off on master data quality
UC-1 oracle match to the rupee
Each type reproduces its sheet / formula
UC-3 freeze test passes; revisions visible
Branded PDF from snapshot; Won/Lost flow works
Master link toggles re-pricing; old quotes still frozen
Pipe, Paper Reel, plant-level COP, reports

Every design question raised during the spec review is now closed. The build proceeds with the assumptions below baked in. None of these are blockers for Phase 0 or Phase 1 — they are stated here so any later change has a recorded baseline.
Still open’; design decisions confirmed with Saurabh
Each row is a closed decision. The Topic column names the area. The Locked
is what the build implements. The Source / file column points to where the rule lives in the build.
Use top_compound.SG for the sandwich volume. Cover and bottom-cover weights still use their own SGs.
Weight-driving. Override flows into carcass thickness in the skim remainder.
No approval gate. Negative or below-threshold GP% shown in red. SEND is not blocked.
Each override is paired with a Top / Bottom selector. Default Top. User can switch to Bottom or apply both.
Two separate dropdowns on the form, both reading from the combined Reel/Packing master with different
Treated as a heavy multiply belt. Hole-punching not modelled in v1.
One-ply belt with a dedicated Belt Rating row,
v1 ships with two roles: Admin (full access) and Sales (quote CRUD, no master writes). Other roles deferred to a Phase-3 ticket.
CUST-{NNNN} runs continuous (no FY reset). ENQ/{FY}/{DDMM}-{NNNN} and QTN/{FY}/{DDMM}-{NNNN}, NNNN resets on FY change. FY in YY-YY, date in DDMM.
Saurabh provides validated master rows (compounds, freight, fabric, COP) at dev kick-off. Import pipeline is ready; build proceeds in parallel until rows arrive.
15.2 Why these are locked, not deferred

Locking each item now gives the team a deterministic build target. If a decision later needs to flip — for example, LD-2 moves from weight-driving to display-only — the change is a single isolated diff against
the assumption recorded here, not a rebuild of dependent logic. The cost of changing a locked decision later is small precisely because it is locked.
15.3 Items that still need a downstream confirmation
Three items are locked for now but expect a short refinement once their belt type or feature is built. None block the start of work.
Confirm Bucket Elevator treatment when that belt type is built in Phase 2. If hole-punching needs to be modelled, add
Confirm Straight Warp one-ply treatment when that belt type is built in Phase 2. If the math needs its own family, register it in 03-belt-type-matrix.md.
Full role matrix lands as a Phase-3 ticket. v1 ships with Admin + Sales only.

Correctness is proven against fixed oracles, not against intuition. Three layers of validation run continuously through the build: the corrected-Excel oracle, a test pack of real past quotes, and edge-case scenarios that exercise the silent-failure surface.
16.1 Layer 1 — The corrected Excel as oracle

CorrectedMultiplyBeltCosting.xlsx is the rupee-for-rupee oracle for the Multiply-with-Breaker engine. It is a clean, master-driven rebuild of the original Multiply costing — every rate is in the Masters tab, every formula in the costing tab reads via VLOOKUP, and the calculated outputs match the legacy Excel’s cached numbers exactly.
Phase 1 acceptance: feed the engine the same inputs as the corrected Excel; the four headline figures (weight, total cost, RMC/m, Standard /m and VD /m) must match to ₹1.
Phase 2 acceptance: as each belt type lands, produce the equivalent ‘corrected sheet’ for that type using the same two-tab pattern (Masters + costing tab). Engine output must match.
Regression: the oracle file is checked into source control. Any engine change re-runs the oracle test pack automatically.
16.2 Layer 2 — Test pack of real past quotes
A curated set of 10 real historic quotes — drawn from the HRS
(HRSMASTERSHEETC2526HRSNEW.xlsx) — with the expected total cost, RMC and quoted rate captured. The test pack covers varied belt types, widths, grades, edge types, and end types.
Required diversity in the test pack (per 06-phasing-plan.md):
At least one Cut-Edge and one
At least one Endless and one Open-End belt — exercises X2.
At least one multi-ply high-strength belt (e.g., EP 1000/5+).
At least one UHR (EPDM) belt — exercises skim-mismatch warning.
At least one export (USD) quote — exercises currency snapshot.
The test pack runs at the end of each phase. A failure does not just flag a bug; it flags a model gap — usually a missing master row or a
Each guard from §11 is a deterministic test case:
§11.1 master lookup failure → archive a compound mid-DRAFT, attempt to SEND → expect
§11.2 skim ↔ cover family mismatch → pick GP skim with HR cover → expect warning banner; quote still composable.
§11.3 negative skim → manufactured input → expect hard block.
§11.5 custom width → request 1350 mm not in master → expect Provisional flag, SEND disabled until Admin approves.
§11.7 short endless → 2 m endless belt → expect
§11.8 below-cost discount → expect red ‘implied GP% negative’ display, quote not auto-blocked.
The freeze contract is so important it deserves its own permanent test:
Create a quote; send it; record RMC, Standard /m, VD /m.
Change every master rate referenced by that quote (cover, fabric, skim, breaker, COP, freight, packing).
Reopen the sent quote → expect the original RMC, Standard /m, VD /m, with no deviation.
Create a revision → expect new numbers based on current masters, and v1 still readable with v1 numbers.
Regenerate the PDF for v1 → expect the v1 snapshot to drive the PDF, not the current masters.
A phase is not done until every column above is green. A regression in any prior phase is a blocker for the current one.
Every claim in this document traces back to a project file. This section is the canonical index — where to look in the project knowledge to verify, extend, or challenge any statement made in §1–§16.
What it backs in this document
Executive summary (§1), the three principles (§2), glossary (§3). Source of the open-questions list now resolved as Locked Decisions in §15.
Bugs Fixed section (§12) in full — every C-, S-, X-numbered finding and the silent-failure list.
Master Data Catalogue (§5) and the database schema (§13). The authoritative source for the compound model,
compatibility, fabric three-tier model, breaker, edge, reel/packing, freight, cost-of-production, belt type, width, ply, customer, currency.
Grade families (GP/AR/HR/FR/OR/COLD/LRR), skim-matching engine, the bonding-risk warning
Belt Type Matrix (§7), the 14 belt types → 5 calculation families,
Calculation Engine (§6) — every canonical formula, every fix to the legacy bug list, the universal one-engine model.
Quotation Lifecycle (§9) — state machine, freeze rule, revision flow, post-quote tracking. The ‘still open’ approval-gate question (OQ-3).
Phased Build Plan (§14) and Validation Strategy (§16) — phase scope, acceptance criteria, build order.
QA oracle definition for Phase 1 (§16.1); the four headline numbers used in UC-1 (§10.1).
Reference for how the team’s flow_Edited.docx maps onto this canonical spec — ~80% of the team’s UI design is adopted; deltas are flagged.
Team’s schema document. Partially superseded by 02-master-architecture.md where the two diverge. Cited in §13.
The QA oracle for Phase 1. UC-1 (§10.1) reproduces its figures rupee-for-rupee. Tabs: Masters (yellow = rates), Multiply with Breaker (blue = inputs, green = lookups, black = calculated).
Legacy 6-sheet Excel — the source of all C-, S-, X-numbered bugs in §12.1 / §12.2 / §12.3. Cited by sheet name and cell reference throughout §12.
(~7000 rows × 108 cols). Source of test-pack rows (§16.2) and the silent-failure findings on
19-sheet import-shape file used to seed the masters. Defines the import contract — column names, units, validation rules — for Phase 0 onboarding.
17.3 How to verify a claim in this document
Read the source-tag line at the head of the section (italic, muted).
Open the cited file from the project knowledge.
Cross-check the specific assertion against the source. Any disagreement → flag it as a discrepancy; do not silently reconcile.
/build_doc.js. Edits should be made to the build script, not to the rendered docx.
Source files are the canonical truth. When a source file changes, this document is regenerated.
Locked decisions (every italic ‘LOCKED’ marker in the underlying files, plus §15 of this doc) must be reflected verbatim. Any future change reopens that decision in §15 first.
